title: Somalia: A State without Settlement | African Arguments
gist: The author argues Somalia is entering a 'state without settlement' era: President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud extended his term, used force against opposition leaders in Mogadishu, and is consolidating power via constitutional rewrites and an OPOV electoral system, leaning on Turkish/Emirati patronage that lowers the cost of escalation - producing a 'shell state' whose juridical sovereignty masks fiscal insolvency, no monopoly of force, and Al-Shabaab as the chief beneficiary.

insights.claim: Somalia is entering a 'state without the settlement' era as deadly clashes in Mogadishu now stem from the federal government securing political control rather than from Al-Shabaab or checkpoint disputes.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: Marks a regime-stability inflection point: state violence is now intra-elite, signalling deeper instability than insurgency alone.
insights.quote: we are entering a new era– a state without the settlement.

insights.claim: President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud overhauled the Provisional Constitution to extend his term past the scheduled May 2026 election date, escalating tensions in Mogadishu.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: Term extension via constitutional rewrite is the central destabiliser; watch for opposition mobilisation.

insights.claim: Government forces including Gorgor special forces, police and presidential guard tried and failed to detain opposition leader Hassan Ali Khaire, with mortar rounds hitting civilian houses and Bakara market.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: Failed coercion against a Hawiye sub-clan leader shows the government's limited control and risk of clan escalation.

insights.claim: Villa Somalia is betting that a wearied, divided international community and the passage of time will render the president's power consolidation irreversible.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: The 'run down the clock' calculus predicts continued stalling over genuine negotiation, sustaining instability.
insights.quote: Villa Somalia is betting that time and attrition will render the president’s consolidation irreversible.

insights.claim: The government overhauled the constitution and introduced a heavily skewed one-person-one-vote (OPOV) electoral system calibrated to enfranchise pro-government constituencies.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: Engineered electoral system signals managed rather than competitive elections, undermining legitimacy.

insights.claim: The Ministry of Defence under Ahmed Fiqi purged the Somali National Army via biometric registration to remove disloyal soldiers, prioritising troops from the president's and PM's sub-clans.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: Clan-based militarisation of the security apparatus raises civil-conflict risk and weakens national army cohesion.

insights.claim: The government leans heavily on Ankara, which has reaped rewards in promised oil, resources and a military base on the Gulf of Aden, while Emirati patronage underpins parallel authority structures.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: External patrons are trading security and resources for influence; oil promises tie Turkey to regime survival.
insights.locator: And as the government's political support

insights.claim: Foreign patronage has dramatically lowered the cost of escalation and the necessity of compromise in Somalia's political marketplace, overturning the prior restraint imposed by the diplomatic corps.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: Removes the guardrails that previously checked elite violence, structurally raising conflict risk.
insights.quote: foreign patronage, dramatically lowering the cost of escalation and the necessity of compromise in Somalia’s marketplace.
insights.locator: Such logic, though, has been upturned

insights.claim: Somalia is a 'shell state' performing sovereignty without substance - the federal government holds no monopoly of force, evidenced by violent attempts to disarm opposition leaders in the capital.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: Frames the gap between juridical and empirical sovereignty as the core structural weakness; statehood may give way to substance or abandonment.
insights.quote: a shell state– a master in the performance of sovereignty without substance.

insights.claim: Somalia's domestic revenue is far outstripped by external budget support, and preferential IMF and World Bank loans are expected to end at the next round of IDA funding, squeezing the state's solvency.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: Looming fiscal cliff is a concrete trigger for potential state collapse, akin to the post-Soviet/American subsidy collapse under Barre.
insights.quote: its domestic revenue far outstripped by its external budget support.

insights.claim: The principal beneficiary of Somalia's protracted dysfunction and the externalisation of security via the AU peacekeeping mission is Al-Shabaab, as foreign-funded forces are deployed against domestic opposition rather than the jihadists controlling much of the hinterland.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive: counterterrorism aid entrenches the very threat it targets, a structural failure of the state-building model.
insights.quote: The principal beneficiary of this protracted dysfunction, as ever, is Al-Shabaab.
insights.locator: But that, too, may soon begin to crack

insights.claim: The consolidation reflects a longer Islamist agenda opposing federalism and favouring strong-man rule, with the president's Muslim Brotherhood faction Damul Jadiid under Education Minister Farah Abdulkadir systematically capturing state institutions.
insights.speaker: Matthew de Waal
insights.so_what: Institutional capture will outlast any individual president, meaning the trajectory persists even with a leadership change.
insights.locator: It is the culmination of another longer agenda
